Massive marketing spend ($63M in pre-launch marketing). Zero product-market fit validation.

sources: Jason Lemkin 'Don't Hire a VP Marketing Until $1M ARR'
First Round 'The Myth of the Marketing ROI'

name: Category Creation Harvested by Competitors
narrative: The company spent years and millions educating the market about a new category. Then a better-funded competitor (or an incumbent) enters the now-validated market, skips the education phase, and captures the customers the pioneer educated. The pioneer gets credit for creating the category. The harvester gets the revenue.

warning_signals.signal: Larger competitor announcing product in your category after 2+ years of your existence
Marketing spend on category education above 50% of total marketing budget
Your blog/content ranking #1 but prospects choosing competitors
Category search volume growing but your win rate flat
Analyst reports naming your category but not making you the clear leader

survival_playbook: Stop funding category education — let competitors pay for it now that the market exists
Aggressively build switching costs: data lock-in, workflow integrations, certifications
Move to a defensive position: own the niche where you have the deepest expertise
Create a community around your product — community is the one thing that can't be copied
File for key patents now before competitors do — even weak patents create friction

famous_failures.detail: Created social networking category. MySpace harvested it. Facebook harvested from MySpace.
Created cloud accounting SMB category in some markets. QuickBooks Online entered after validation.

name: Cloud Infrastructure Cost Explosion
narrative: AWS, GCP, or Azure costs are growing faster than revenue. The architecture was designed for rapid development, not cost efficiency. Unoptimised queries, over-provisioned instances, and forgotten services generate bills that shock the team every month. Infrastructure costs reach 40-60% of gross revenue, making the business model impossible. The technical debt of cost inefficiency is as fatal as the technical debt of code quality.

warning_signals.signal: Cloud costs growing faster than MRR month-over-month
Gross margin declining below 50% due to infrastructure spend
Cloud bill surprise over 20% above forecast in 2+ consecutive months
No per-customer infrastructure cost tracking in place
Data storage costs alone exceeding 10% of revenue

survival_playbook: Do a cloud cost audit today — identify the top 5 cost centres and their business justification
Implement per-customer cost attribution — know your true gross margin per customer
Right-size all instances: most teams over-provision by 3-5x on default settings
Move frequently-accessed data to cheaper storage tiers; archive infrequently accessed data
Negotiate enterprise discounts with your cloud provider — available at $100K+/year spend

famous_failures.detail: BI SaaS with massive cloud costs. Infrastructure spend was 60%+ of revenue at IPO. Stock fell 85%.
GPU compute costs at $50K+/month burn multiple AI startups before revenue scales.

sources: Corey Quinn cloud cost optimisation research

name: Outbound-Only Revenue Ceiling
narrative: The entire revenue engine is outbound: cold email, cold calls, LinkedIn outreach. There is no inbound. No SEO. No word of mouth. No brand. Outbound works until it doesn't — response rates decline as inboxes saturate, lists exhaust, and sales team scales linearly with revenue. The company cannot grow faster than it can hire AEs, and it cannot hire AEs faster than outbound ROI allows.

warning_signals.signal: Cold outreach reply rates declining 2+ months in a row
Inbound pipeline below 10% of total pipeline
Sales team productivity (revenue per AE) declining
No organic search traffic or referral traffic to website
Customer NPS too low to generate referrals

survival_playbook: Start content marketing immediately — build SEO assets that compound over time
Implement a formal referral programme — happy customers are your cheapest AEs
Create a free tier or freemium product that generates inbound interest
Invest in community building — a community generates perpetual inbound
Fix NPS first — if customers don't refer you, no inbound strategy works

famous_failures.detail: Many outbound-only SaaS companies plateaued at $2-5M ARR and couldn't break through without inbound.
Sales email tool. Heavy outbound model. Acquired by Vendasta after growth plateau.

name: B2B2C Alignment Failure
narrative: The company sells to businesses (B) who deploy to their end users (C). The business buyer and the end user want fundamentally different things. The buyer wants cost reduction, compliance, and reporting. The end user wants ease of use, speed, and delight. The company optimises for whoever pays (the business) and the end users revolt — low adoption, workarounds, and eventually the business cancels because their users won't use the product.

warning_signals.signal: Product purchased by IT/procurement but not used by intended end users
End user NPS dramatically lower than buyer NPS
Renewal conversations citing 'low adoption' as primary concern
Buyers asking for usage reports because they don't know if it's being used
Shadow IT: end users finding workarounds outside your product

survival_playbook: Design for the end user first — if they love it, the buyer will renew without being asked
Build adoption dashboards that buyers can share with their teams internally
Create an end-user champion programme — internal advocates are your renewal insurance
Measure and report DAU/MAU to buyers proactively — show usage before they ask
Build onboarding flows for end users, not just admin setup flows for buyers

famous_failures.detail: IT bought it, employees didn't use it. Slack won by being end-user-first.
Procurement teams loved it. Operations staff refused to use it. Classic B2B2C alignment failure.

name: Convertible Note Stack Collapse
narrative: Multiple convertible note rounds have accumulated with various valuation caps, discount rates, and maturity dates. When the equity round finally comes, the notes convert at different prices and create a massively dilutive event. The cap table becomes too complex for new investors to navigate. Or the notes mature without an equity round, become payable immediately, and the company cannot pay. The flexibility of notes becomes a liability.

warning_signals.signal: More than 3 outstanding convertible notes from different investors
Earliest note maturing within 12 months without clear equity round
Investors asking for cap table clarity before investing
Pro-rata rights and MFN clauses creating complexity in new round negotiations
Note holders requesting equity conversion at unfavourable terms

survival_playbook: Consolidate all notes into a single, clean SAFE before approaching equity investors
Negotiate maturity date extensions proactively — do not wait until the deadline
Model the cap table post-conversion under multiple equity round scenarios — know the math
Engage a startup-specialised attorney to renegotiate the most onerous note terms
Communicate transparently with note holders — surprise debt calls are company-killers

famous_failures.detail: YC estimates 15-20% of struggling companies have cap table complexity as a contributing factor to failure.
Complex note structure contributed to inability to raise follow-on. $25M burned.

sources: SAFE note best practices from Y Combinator

name: Third-Party Data Dependency Collapse
narrative: The product's core value depends on data licensed or scraped from a third party. The data provider changes their terms, raises prices by 10x, or blocks access entirely. The company cannot deliver its core value proposition overnight. Most data dependency companies discover this during a fundraise when investors ask 'what happens if they cut you off?' — and there is no good answer.

warning_signals.signal: Core product functionality dependent on single data provider API
Data provider announcing policy changes affecting access
Legal notices about scraping or terms of service violations
Investors declining citing data dependency as existential risk
Data provider announcing competing product in your category

survival_playbook: Build a proprietary data moat immediately — user-generated data, proprietary collection, partnerships
Diversify data sources so no single provider represents more than 30% of your data
Formalise licensing agreements with legal protections against arbitrary termination
Build data partnerships where you provide value back to the data owner — not just extraction
Create a 30-day business continuity plan for if your primary data source disappears tomorrow

famous_failures.detail: Multiple fintech data aggregators destroyed when Plaid, Yodlee changed API terms.
Entire ecosystem of Twitter clients destroyed overnight when API pricing changed 100x.

name: Two-Tier Compensation Collapse
narrative: Early employees accepted below-market salaries in exchange for equity. Later employees are hired at market rate. The compensation gap becomes visible. Early employees discover they're paid 30-40% less than newer colleagues for the same work. Resentment builds. Your most loyal and most knowledgeable people leave. New hires don't have the context to replace them. The institutional knowledge walks out the door.

warning_signals.signal: Early employees raising compensation concerns in 1:1s
Salary spreadsheet leaked or discovered — triggering internal comparisons
Above-average tenure employees leaving for 'better compensation'
Glassdoor reviews citing pay inequity
Cluster of early-team departures within a 90-day window

survival_playbook: Conduct a compensation audit: identify every case where tenure and contribution diverge
Create a transparent levelling system so comp decisions are explicable, not arbitrary
Give early employees a market-rate salary adjustment — the loyalty premium should show in equity, not a pay cut
Communicate proactively: tell your founding team you're aware of the gap and have a plan
Accelerate vesting for key early employees who are approaching a cliff — retention is the goal

famous_failures.detail: Documented early-team compensation tensions. Resolved with explicit bands and market corrections.
Post-Series B hiring at market rates routinely triggers early employee departures at companies without comp bands.

name: Contractor Misclassification Liability
narrative: The company classified workers as independent contractors to avoid payroll taxes, benefits, and employment law obligations. Labour authorities reclassify them as employees. Back taxes, penalties, benefits liability, and legal costs can exceed $1M+. The gig economy model that made unit economics work suddenly doesn't. In California and the EU especially, this is an existential compliance risk.

warning_signals.signal: Contractor workforce representing more than 30% of operational headcount
Labour authority audit notice or worker complaint received
Contractors working exclusively for the company with no other clients
Company controlling how contractors work (hours, tools, location)
Similar-model companies facing regulatory action in your market

survival_playbook: Conduct an employment law audit of every contractor relationship before regulators do
Engage an employment attorney familiar with AB5 (CA) and equivalent local laws
For contractors at risk: convert to employees or restructure to genuine contractor model
Document contractor independence clearly: multiple clients, own tools, own schedule
Never misclassify to save on payroll tax — the back-liability always exceeds the savings

famous_failures.detail: Spent $200M on ballot initiative to survive contractor classification issue.
Shut down citing contractor misclassification lawsuits as a primary factor.

name: Demo-to-Close Gap
narrative: The product demos beautifully. Every demo gets a positive response. But nobody buys. Prospects go quiet after the demo. The pipeline looks healthy but conversion to close is below 10%. The product delivers a compelling 45-minute experience that doesn't translate to day-to-day value. Sales team blames the product. Product team blames sales. The truth is usually a broken post-demo process, unclear buying journey, or a product that solves the demo problem, not the actual problem.

warning_signals.signal: Demo-to-trial conversion above 60% but trial-to-paid below 15%
Prospects going silent after positive demo calls
Average sales cycle extending beyond 90 days without clear buying milestones
'Not the right time' and 'budget concerns' dominating loss reasons
No clear post-demo follow-up process or mutual action plan

survival_playbook: Record every demo and watch 10 of them — identify exactly where energy drops
Create a mutual action plan for every deal: specific milestones, dates, and owner on each side
Build a trial that delivers aha moment in 5 minutes, not 5 hours
Follow up every demo with a 1-page business case tailored to their specific situation
Add a mandatory 'next step' at the end of every demo — no demo ends without a scheduled next call

famous_failures.detail: Classic pattern in Gartner sales process research — demo enthusiasm rarely correlates with close rate.
Documented post-demo gap issue. Fixed with mutual success plans and mandatory POC structure.

sources: Winning by Design sales process research

name: Integration Hell
narrative: The product requires 5+ integrations to deliver its core value. Each integration is a point of failure: credentials expire, APIs change, webhooks break. The support team spends 60% of time on integration issues. Customer NPS is dragged down by reliability problems unrelated to the core product. Enterprise prospects require 6 months of integration work before going live. The product's value is hostage to every third-party system it touches.

warning_signals.signal: Integration-related support tickets above 40% of total support volume
Time-to-value extending due to integration setup requirements
Enterprise prospects requiring integration certification before purchase
NPS scores correlating with number of integrations customer is using
Engineering spending 30%+ of time maintaining integration reliability

survival_playbook: Identify the 3 integrations that drive 80% of activation — harden these, deprecate the rest
Build a dedicated integration infrastructure team — treat integrations as a product, not an afterthought
Create an integration health dashboard for customers — proactive failure notification
Consider using a middleware platform (Zapier, Workato) for long-tail integrations
Charge for integration setup and maintenance — it creates accountability and revenue

famous_failures.detail: Integration reliability issues drove NPS down during rapid growth phase.
Multiple integration platform startups killed by reliability issues before achieving scale.

name: Founding Team Domain Gap
narrative: The founding team built a product for a domain they don't deeply understand. They learned about the problem from interviews, not lived experience. The product solves the surface-level problem but misses the deep workflow nuances. Domain experts who evaluate the product immediately find fundamental flaws. The team spends 3 years discovering what an industry insider would have known on day one.

warning_signals.signal: Customers saying 'you clearly haven't worked in our industry' repeatedly
Domain experts declining to use or endorse the product
Sales cycle dominated by educating founders about the domain
Product roadmap constantly reactive to customer education, not proactive
Competitor founded by domain insider winning deals the team should close

survival_playbook: Hire a domain expert as an advisor with equity — their pattern recognition is your shortcut
Do 50 hours of job shadowing in your target customer's workflow before next product sprint
Create a customer advisory board of 5 domain experts who review product decisions monthly
Consider recruiting a domain expert as co-founder even at this stage — better dilution than failure
Stop building features until you can pass a domain knowledge test with a power user

famous_failures.detail: Built by engineers without deep healthcare workflow understanding. Used by 0.1% of target audience.
$3.9B investment in healthcare. Lacked healthcare domain expertise in leadership.

name: Seasonal Revenue Concentration
narrative: The business is structurally dependent on a single season or event cycle. Q4 is 60% of annual revenue. Summer is dead. Conventions or trade shows generate all pipeline. The company has 90 days per year to make its number and 270 days to wait and burn. Cash management becomes impossible. The team over-hires for the busy season and can't afford to keep them in the slow season.

warning_signals.signal: Q4 or single quarter above 40% of annual revenue
Burn rate exceeding revenue by 3x+ in off-peak months
Cash balance hitting dangerous lows before seasonal revenue arrives
Team seasonal hiring and firing creating culture damage
Investors concerned about seasonal cash flow in due diligence

survival_playbook: Build counter-cyclical revenue streams for the off-season — services, training, consulting
Negotiate payment terms that smooth cash flow: annual pre-payments with discounts, monthly billing
Maintain 6 months of operating expenses in cash reserve before peak season ends
Avoid permanent hires for seasonal capacity — use contractors or part-time for peak demand
Find customers whose seasonality is opposite to yours — smooth the revenue curve

famous_failures.detail: Black Friday/holiday-dependent commerce tools routinely fail in Q1 cash crunches.
Season-dependent analytics tools burn through cash during off-season.

sources: CB Insights seasonal business failure analysis

name: Influencer Marketing Collapse
narrative: The company's growth depends on a small number of influencer relationships. When those influencers move to a competing product, lose their audience, or demand higher fees, acquisition collapses. Influencer-acquired customers are also lower-quality — they came for the influencer, not the product, and churn when the novelty wears off. The company built audience for someone else.

warning_signals.signal: More than 50% of new acquisition attributed to 3 or fewer influencers
Influencer-acquired cohorts churning at 2x the baseline rate
Key influencer announcing partnership with competing product
Influencer fee demands increasing without commensurate ROI improvement
Organic/direct acquisition flat despite growing influencer spend

survival_playbook: Build direct audience ownership immediately: email list, community, owned social
Diversify to minimum 10 different influencers — no single one above 15% of attributed acquisition
Measure influencer ROI by 90-day retention, not clicks — kill relationships with bad retention
Create an affiliate programme to scale to 100s of micro-influencers rather than 5 macro ones
Build product virality that doesn't depend on paid influencers — word of mouth is better

famous_failures.detail: Entirely influencer-dependent marketing for zero-substance product. Fraud charges followed.
DTC brands dependent on Facebook/Instagram influencers destroyed when iOS 14 privacy killed attribution.

name: AI Model API Dependency
narrative: The product is an AI wrapper: the core value is delivered by calling OpenAI, Anthropic, or another LLM API. The model provider raises prices, changes capabilities, degrades performance, or builds a competing product. The wrapper company has no defensibility — the model provider can replicate their interface in weeks. Investors increasingly refuse to fund pure AI wrappers without proprietary data or workflow moats.

warning_signals.signal: Core product functionality entirely dependent on one LLM API
Model provider announcing products that compete with your use case
API cost increasing faster than revenue as usage scales
Investors asking 'what happens if OpenAI builds this?' with no good answer
Model capability changes breaking product behaviour with no warning

survival_playbook: Build a proprietary data layer that improves with usage — your data moat is your defensibility
Integrate 3+ model providers so you can switch — no single API should be irreplaceable
Focus on the workflow and UX layer — that is harder to replicate than the AI call
Create fine-tuned models on your proprietary data — small models trained on your data beat generic APIs
Build network effects: the more users, the better the product — something no model API can copy

famous_failures.detail: ChatGPT launch directly cannibalized AI writing wrapper category. Valuation fell from $1.5B.
AI copywriting tools built on GPT-3/4 wrappers struggled after ChatGPT direct access launched.

name: Long Payback Period Trap
narrative: CAC payback period is 36+ months. The company is cash-negative on every new customer for 3 years. This creates a structural cash drain: the faster you grow, the more cash you consume. The business model only works if you can raise perpetually. When capital markets tighten, payback periods above 24 months become unacceptable to investors — and the company cannot survive without continuous external capital.

warning_signals.signal: CAC payback period above 24 months and not improving
Gross margin below 60% making payback period improvement structural
Revenue growth requiring proportional capital injection to sustain
Investors requesting payback period projections and declining based on answers
New customer acquisition accelerating cash consumption, not reducing it

survival_playbook: Reduce CAC: focus only on the 1-2 channels with fastest payback, kill the rest
Increase ACV: raise prices for new customers — payback compresses immediately
Reduce time-to-first-value: faster activation means faster expansion and earlier payback
Move to annual prepay billing — customers who prepay improve your payback period structurally
Set a hard goal: payback period below 18 months before next fundraise

famous_failures.detail: 36+ month payback on hardware + subscription. Works in low-rate environments, fails in high-rate ones.

famous_failures.outcome: IPO at deep discount then taken private
famous_failures.detail: Mattress DTC. CAC payback period too long to generate positive unit economics at scale.

name: Infrastructure Over-Engineering
narrative: Engineers build for 10 million users when you have 100. Kubernetes clusters, event sourcing, CQRS, and distributed message queues are installed before a single paying customer exists. The architecture is impressive and impossible to ship features on. Six months of engineering effort builds infrastructure nobody needed yet. By the time product-market fit is needed, runway is gone.

warning_signals.signal: Infrastructure discussion dominating product planning meetings
Feature delivery delayed by infrastructure dependencies
Engineering team headcount disproportionate to customer count
CTO citing scalability as reason for slow feature delivery
Less than 50% of sprint velocity on customer-facing features

survival_playbook: Mandate: shipping speed is more important than scalability until $1M ARR
Use managed services (Firebase, Supabase, Railway) that scale automatically — don't build infra
Establish a rule: no infrastructure component for a scale problem you don't have yet
Ship the feature in the simplest possible way first — optimise only when you have the problem
Remember: you can always optimise working code; you cannot fundraise on perfect architecture

famous_failures.detail: YC partners regularly cite over-engineering as reason companies run out of money before finding PMF.
CB Insights documents engineering paralysis as a contributing factor in 18% of early-stage failures.

sources: Paul Graham 'Do Things That Don't Scale'
Sam Altman 'How to Start a Startup'

name: Multi-Product Premature Expansion
narrative: The company launches a second product before the first is profitable. Each product requires its own GTM motion, support infrastructure, engineering attention, and customer success resources. Focus divides. The first product stagnates. The second product lacks the resources to gain traction. The company ends up with two mediocre products instead of one great one.

warning_signals.signal: Second product launch before core product reaches $1M ARR
Burn rate increasing without proportional revenue increase
Core product feature roadmap slowing due to second product resource diversion
Sales team unsure which product to lead with in conversations
First product NPS declining as team attention shifts

survival_playbook: Shut down the second product if core product is not yet default alive
Establish a rule: no new product until core product has 18+ months runway and 110%+ NRR
Build product #2 as an add-on or upsell to existing customers — not a separate motion
If second product is necessary: build a completely separate team with separate P&L
The best second product is usually a deeper version of your first product, not a different product

famous_failures.detail: Launched HEY while Basecamp growth was flat. Divided team and attention.
Multiple product expansions while core music streaming product was losing to Spotify.

sources: Jason Lemkin 'One Product Rule for SaaS'

name: CTO-CPO Strategic Misalignment
narrative: The CTO and CPO have fundamentally different views of what the product should be and how fast it should move. The CTO wants to rebuild for scale before shipping more features. The CPO wants to ship features before optimising. Each sub-team aligns to their leader. Cross-team projects get stuck in standoffs. Customers notice the resulting incoherence — features that don't work together, bugs that linger, promises that slip.

warning_signals.signal: Product and engineering roadmaps diverging — different priorities
Cross-functional projects stalling due to prioritisation disagreements
Employees reporting to different leaders on same project with conflicting direction
CEO spending significant time mediating CTO/CPO conflicts
Customer-facing delivery slipping due to internal disputes

survival_playbook: Establish clear decision rights: product defines what, engineering defines how
Create a joint planning process: CTO and CPO must agree on quarterly priorities before the quarter starts
If irresolvable: one of them must go — usually the one who is wrong for the company's current stage
Bring in a CEO-level mediator (board member or executive coach) before it becomes a resignation
Create a joint north star metric that both roles are measured against — alignment follows incentives

famous_failures.detail: CTO/CPO strategic split between consumer and enterprise direction delayed critical pivot.
First Round's analysis of Series B failures cites executive misalignment in 40% of cases.

name: Incumbent Awakening
narrative: The startup disrupted a market segment that the incumbent initially ignored as too small or too risky. The startup grew to the point where the incumbent notices and responds. With superior distribution, brand trust, and existing customer relationships, the incumbent's 'good enough' product is sufficient for the majority of the market. The startup is left with the early adopter segment who can't sustain a venture-scale business.

warning_signals.signal: Incumbent announcing product directly competing with your core offering
Enterprise prospects citing 'we already have this with [incumbent]' in deals
Incumbent bundling competing functionality into existing contracts for free
Win rate declining specifically against incumbent-connected prospects
Customers asking about incumbent's roadmap before committing to renewal

survival_playbook: Move upmarket immediately — serve customers the incumbent won't serve (too complex, too niche, too demanding)
Build deep workflow integrations the incumbent won't build due to internal politics
Win on speed, attention, and partnership — offer what a large company structurally cannot
Find the underserved segment within your market where the incumbent's product is weakest
Consider whether a strategic acquisition by the incumbent is the right outcome now

famous_failures.detail: Intuit (QuickBooks) responded aggressively to Xero's US entry. Xero US growth stalled.

famous_failures.outcome: Sold to Salesforce under market pressure
famous_failures.detail: Microsoft bundled Teams into Office 365. Slack sold to Salesforce for $27.7B to survive.

name: Complexity Creep
narrative: The product started as something elegant and simple that a user could learn in 10 minutes. Three years and 200 features later, it takes 3 days of training. New users are overwhelmed. The product has grown to accommodate every edge case of every customer. The core workflow — the reason anyone signed up — is buried under layers of optional features. New user activation collapses. Existing users stay out of switching cost, not love.

warning_signals.signal: New user activation time increasing year-over-year
NPS of users under 6 months tenure significantly lower than long-tenure users
Support ticket volume growing faster than user base
Sales team requiring 2+ hour demos to show core value
Users describing product as 'powerful but overwhelming'

survival_playbook: Create a 'simple mode' that hides all advanced features — let users opt into complexity
Identify the 20% of features used by 80% of users — make those the default experience
Run a ruthless feature audit: deprecate anything used by fewer than 10% of active users
Rebuild onboarding to deliver aha moment in under 5 minutes, ignoring all other features
Hire a Head of Product Design specifically to simplify — not just ship new features

famous_failures.outcome: Sold at fraction of peak valuation
famous_failures.detail: Classic complexity creep. Simple note-taker became unusable Swiss Army knife. Users migrated to Notion and Apple Notes.
Desktop version became so complex that Intuit had to rebuild from scratch for cloud.

name: AI Compliance Regulatory Block
narrative: The company built an AI product in a regulated domain — healthcare, finance, hiring, or credit — without building the compliance infrastructure required. The EU AI Act, FDA SaMD guidelines, EEOC algorithmic accountability rules, or CFPB fair lending requirements block the product in key markets. Enterprise customers cannot deploy an AI product that lacks the required risk assessments and audit trails.

warning_signals.signal: Enterprise prospects requesting AI risk assessments and audit logs you can't provide
Regulatory body issuing guidance that directly applies to your AI system
Legal team flagging AI outputs in high-risk categories (hiring, credit, health)
EU market access blocked due to AI Act high-risk category requirements
Competitor achieving regulatory approval you don't have, winning enterprise deals

survival_playbook: Classify your AI system under applicable regulations — EU AI Act, FDA, EEOC — before expanding
Build explainability and audit logging into the AI pipeline from the start — retrofitting is 5x harder
Engage a regulatory counsel specialising in AI law before product launch, not after
Consider a 'human in the loop' design that reduces regulatory risk tier in most frameworks
Pursue CE marking or FDA clearance proactively — it becomes a competitive advantage

famous_failures.detail: Facial analysis hiring tool. FTC scrutiny and EEOC concerns forced removal of core feature.
FDA SaMD classification requirements blocked multiple AI diagnostic tools from US market.

name: Insider-Only Funding Dependency
narrative: Existing investors keep leading bridge rounds with no new external lead. On the surface this looks like strong investor support. In reality it signals: no new investor has done diligence and found the company fundable at this stage. Each insider bridge extends life but doesn't fix the underlying problem. When insiders run out of capacity or patience, the company has no external momentum to fall back on.

warning_signals.signal: Last 2+ rounds were insider-only with no new lead investor
External investors declining after full diligence process
Insider investors delaying commitment on next round
Metrics not hitting milestones promised at last round
Insider investor fund lifecycle nearing end (10-year fund, year 8+)

survival_playbook: Get an honest answer from lead investor: 'Would you lead this round if you didn't already own equity?'
Ask each insider: 'What metric change would make you comfortable participating with new money alongside you?'
Find one external investor — even a small check — to create external validation for the round
Consider a complete reset: down round with new lead who resets expectations and brings fresh energy
If insiders won't commit: take that as your signal to dramatically cut burn to default alive

famous_failures.detail: Multiple insider rounds from SoftBank. No new external validation. Shut down 2020.

famous_failures.company: Many SoftBank Vision Fund portfolio companies
famous_failures.detail: Insider-only follow-ons without external validation masked fundamental business problems.

sources: Mark Suster 'Both Sides of the Table' insider round analysis
CB Insights late-stage funding pattern research

name: Land-and-Abandon Customer Success Failure
narrative: The sales team closes deals but there is no customer success motion. Customers go live, get minimal support, and slowly disengage. Expansion revenue is zero because nobody is having business review conversations. Customers renew out of inertia at minimum contract value and churn at the first renewal where they actually review ROI. The company keeps acquiring new customers to replace the ones slowly leaving.

warning_signals.signal: Net Revenue Retention below 90%
Expansion revenue below 10% of total MRR
No assigned customer success contact for accounts above $10K ACV
First renewal churn rate above 25%
Customers citing 'lack of support' as churn reason

survival_playbook: Assign a CSM to every account above $5K ACV immediately — this is table stakes, not a luxury
Implement quarterly business reviews for every enterprise customer as a non-negotiable process
Build a health score: login frequency, feature adoption, support ticket volume — flag at-risk accounts
Compensate CSMs on NRR and expansion, not just renewal rate
Create an executive sponsor programme: your CXO owns the relationship with their CXO

famous_failures.detail: Enterprise social software. No customer success motion. High churn as Slack arrived.
Viral enterprise adoption without CS support. Couldn't monetise at scale without Microsoft distribution.

name: Cohort Decay Acceleration
narrative: Each new customer cohort retains worse than the previous one. Month-1 cohort retained at 80%. Month-12 cohort retains at 60%. Month-24 cohort retains at 40%. The company is expanding into lower-quality market segments or its product quality is degrading as it scales. The aggregate churn rate looks manageable but the trend predicts structural collapse.

warning_signals.signal: Most recent 3-month cohort retaining 10+ percentage points below 12-month ago cohort
ICP definition expanding to less-qualified segments to maintain growth rate
Win rate increasing while retention rate declining — selling to wrong customers to hit quota
Sales discounting increasing to close deals that previously closed at full price
Customer success team noting new customers have different (harder) use cases

survival_playbook: Build cohort retention charts and present them to the full leadership team — face the trend
Audit recent closed deals: are you selling to the wrong ICP to hit growth targets?
Tighten ICP qualification criteria even if it means slower growth
Build a customer fit score before selling — disqualify prospects who match low-retention characteristics
Fix the product issues that are causing increasing churn in new cohorts — don't mask with acquisition

famous_failures.detail: Each merchant cohort performed worse. The model structurally decayed as market saturated.
Credit quality per cohort deteriorated as origination volumes grew. Systematic cohort decay.

name: Vendor Lock-In Trap
narrative: The company's infrastructure is deeply coupled to a single cloud provider's proprietary services. When that provider raises prices, changes terms, or discontinues a service, migration is a 12-18 month engineering project — longer than available runway. Alternatively, a competing cloud provider offers pricing that would save $500K/year, but switching is impossible without a full rebuild.

warning_signals.signal: Core product using 5+ proprietary cloud services that have no open-source equivalent
Cloud provider announcing deprecation of services you depend on
Pricing renegotiation blocked by technical lock-in
Enterprise prospects requiring multi-cloud deployment you can't provide
Cloud costs representing 30%+ of COGS with no alternative

survival_playbook: Audit your dependency graph — list every proprietary service and its open-source alternative
Architect new features with portability in mind — wrap proprietary services in abstraction layers
Renegotiate pricing as a bundled contract — cloud providers discount 30-50% for committed usage
Build a 24-month migration plan even if you don't execute it — having the plan gives negotiating leverage
For new infrastructure decisions: default to open standards, use proprietary only where genuinely superior

famous_failures.detail: 1M+ apps dependent on Parse. 30-day migration window caused mass product deaths.
Committed to GCP proprietary services during startup credits, faced 10x pricing post-credits.

name: Marketing Before PMF
narrative: Founders hire a Head of Marketing and ramp ad spend before achieving product-market fit. Users arrive, don't find value, and churn. The marketing budget effectively funds a failed PMF experiment at 10x the cost of a targeted outreach experiment. Every dollar spent on paid acquisition before PMF is money that could have extended runway to find what actually works.

warning_signals.signal: Marketing spend above $20K/month with churn above 10%
Paid acquisition bringing users who churn faster than organic users
CAC increasing while retention metrics decline — buying bad-fit customers
Head of Marketing hired before 50 retained paying customers exist

survival_playbook: Stop all paid acquisition immediately until month-3 retention exceeds 40%
Founder should do all sales manually until 10 identical deals can be closed with the same pitch
Fire the Head of Marketing if hired pre-PMF — you need a growth hacker, not a brand marketer
Use the saved budget to extend runway for 3 more months of PMF iteration
The only pre-PMF marketing worth paying for: content that attracts the exact ICP organically

famous_failures.detail: $41M raised, $1M+ in marketing pre-launch. Product had no retention. Marketing spend accelerated the burn.
Standard pattern documented in Andreessen Horowitz consumer portfolio research.

name: Social Platform Reach Collapse
narrative: The business was built on organic reach from TikTok, Instagram, or YouTube. Algorithm changes, account suspensions, or platform policy updates destroy reach overnight. The company has no email list, no owned community, and no alternative acquisition channel. Organic reach that previously generated $200K/month in revenue drops to $5K. The company has no way to reach its own audience.

warning_signals.signal: More than 60% of traffic/acquisition from a single social platform
Organic reach declining 20%+ month-over-month without explanation
Email list smaller than 5% of social following
Platform announcing algorithm changes in content category
Account warning, shadow ban, or temporary suspension received

survival_playbook: Start building your email list today — add a capture mechanism to every content touchpoint
Never let any social platform have more than 30% of your audience distribution
Build a community on owned infrastructure (Discord, Circle, email newsletter)
Create content formats that work across multiple platforms simultaneously
Treat social platforms as rented land — build on owned channels as your primary home

famous_failures.detail: Apple iOS 14 privacy update destroyed Facebook attribution. Brands built on Facebook ads saw 40-60% revenue drops.
TikTok ban discussions in US 2023-2024 put platform-dependent businesses at existential risk.

name: Data Quality Collapse
narrative: The company's product or analytics are built on corrupted, duplicated, or inconsistently defined data. Executives make decisions on reports that are wrong. Customers receive incorrect data from the product. Customer success can't identify at-risk accounts because health scores are inaccurate. By the time the data quality issue surfaces, it's usually during a missed forecast or a customer escalation — both with severe consequences.

warning_signals.signal: Reports from different systems giving different numbers for same metric
Customer complaints about data accuracy increasing
Data team spending more than 50% of time on data cleaning vs. analysis
Leadership meetings derailing into debates about which numbers are correct
Customer trust damaged by a high-profile data accuracy incident

survival_playbook: Appoint a data owner — one person accountable for the quality of each critical metric
Build a data contract: define every metric in writing, including formula, source, and owner
Implement data quality monitoring that alerts on anomalies before customers or executives see them
Do a full data audit — identify all sources of duplication and inconsistency before building on top
Invest in dbt or similar data transformation tooling — treat data as code with version control

famous_failures.detail: Bad data and faulty software caused $440M trading loss in 45 minutes. Company sold.
Customer data accuracy issues are the #1 cause of churn in analytics/BI products per G2 reviews.

name: Pivot to AI Wrapper
narrative: The original product isn't working. Someone at the company discovers that adding AI/LLM features creates excitement in demos. The team pivots to become an 'AI-powered' version of the original product without fixing the underlying PMF problem. The new AI features are a thin wrapper over GPT-4. Churn continues for the same reasons. The company has added complexity without adding differentiation.

warning_signals.signal: AI features being added before understanding why original product churned
AI pivot announcement not changing churn rate
Demo improves but close rate does not improve after AI features
Original customers not adopting AI features despite availability
AI features adding cost without adding proportional revenue

survival_playbook: Diagnose why the original product churned before adding any AI — AI doesn't fix product-market fit
If adding AI: make sure it solves a specific, validated customer pain — not a demo pain
Test AI features with 10 existing customers before building — do they actually use it?
AI features require proprietary data to create defensibility — what data do you have that others don't?
Never pivot to AI to solve a fundraising problem — investors can spot AI washing

famous_failures.outcome: Various — AI pivot not fixing churn
famous_failures.detail: Dozens of SaaS companies added 'AI-powered' to their branding. Churn rates unchanged.
AI features initially seen as bolt-on. Required deep integration work to show genuine value.

name: Friends and Family Round Structural Problems
narrative: The company raised its first capital from friends and family at informal terms. The notes have unclear terms, no pro-rata rights, no standard SAFE structure, and emotional investors who don't behave as professional investors. When professional investors arrive, the messy cap table and emotionally invested early shareholders create friction. Alternatively, the F&F round was so large it set an implicit valuation that professional investors won't validate.

warning_signals.signal: Friends and family investors attempting to influence product decisions
Informal notes without standard SAFE or convertible note terms
Professional investors citing 'cap table concerns' in due diligence
Early investor relations consuming founder time disproportionately
F&F round size implying valuation that professionals won't accept

survival_playbook: Always use standard YC SAFE or convertible note — never informal agreements with family
Set expectations explicitly: this is a high-risk investment, likely to be zero — say it in writing
Keep F&F round below $200K to avoid valuation anchoring problems at professional raise
Do not give board seats or information rights to F&F investors
Bring a startup attorney to structure F&F round even if it seems unnecessary at $50K

famous_failures.detail: YC application data shows F&F round cap table problems delay or block professional raises in ~20% of cases.
Multiple cases of F&F investors suing founders over informal promise disputes — legal costs alone kill companies.

name: Support Cost Collapse
narrative: The product is so complex or unreliable that support costs eat into gross margin. Each new customer requires 10 hours of support in their first 30 days. The support team grows linearly with customers. Gross margin collapses from 80% to 50% as support headcount scales. The product's complexity makes self-service impossible. The company is providing a managed service at SaaS pricing.

warning_signals.signal: Support headcount growing at same rate as customer count
Support cost per customer exceeding 15% of ACV
Gross margin declining despite revenue growth
Support ticket volume growing faster than user base
Customers citing 'responsive support' as main reason for staying — not product value

survival_playbook: Build self-service documentation so thorough that 80% of support tickets become searchable answers
Implement in-app contextual help — answer questions at the point of confusion, not in a ticket system
Classify every support ticket by root cause — top 5 causes drive 80% of volume, fix those in product
Create a community forum where power users answer questions — peer support is free
Set a target: support cost below 5% of ACV — measure it and drive it down quarterly

famous_failures.detail: SMB customers required disproportionate support. Forced move upmarket to maintain margins.
Support cost structure preventing path to profitability is a common acqui-hire driver.

name: Trade Show Pipeline Dependency
narrative: All meaningful pipeline is generated at 3-4 annual trade shows. The company budgets $200K+ per show, staffs a large booth, and generates 80% of annual pipeline from those events. Between shows, the pipeline dries up. COVID-19 or event cancellation for any reason eliminates the entire pipeline for that year. The company has no repeatable year-round acquisition engine.

warning_signals.signal: More than 50% of pipeline attributed to trade shows/events
Quarter with no major event having dramatically less pipeline than event quarters
Sales team idle or below target between events
Event costs growing faster than attributed pipeline revenue
Major industry event cancellation or format change announced

survival_playbook: Build a year-round inbound engine — content, SEO, and webinars that generate pipeline every week
Use trade show leads to build a database for year-round outbound — follow up 6x not 1x
Start a digital event strategy — webinars and virtual summits at 10% of physical event cost
Set a rule: no single event category above 25% of annual pipeline
Build partnerships with distribution channels that generate pipeline continuously, not seasonally

famous_failures.detail: COVID-19 event cancellation in 2020 caused immediate pipeline collapse for event-dependent companies.
Medical device companies with surgical conference dependency saw 60%+ pipeline drops in 2020.

name: Wrong Problem Framing
narrative: The company built the right solution to the wrong problem. Customers have the symptom the founders identified, but the root cause is different — and the product addresses the symptom, not the cause. When customers use the product and the underlying problem persists, they churn. Discovery interviews confirmed the symptom. Nobody dug deep enough to find the real problem. Building the right thing for the wrong reason is as fatal as building the wrong thing.

warning_signals.signal: Customers saying product 'helps but doesn't solve it'
Users engaged with product but problem metrics not improving
Customers asking for adjacent features that address a different problem than yours
High trial activation but low sustained usage after novelty phase
NPS of 30-40: users like product but don't recommend it

survival_playbook: Do 20 '5 Whys' interviews with your best AND worst retained customers
Ask: 'What were you doing before our product? What's different now that you use it?'
Identify the metric in your customer's business that your product should move — are you moving it?
Stop measuring product metrics (DAU, sessions) and start measuring customer outcome metrics
Reframe your product around the outcome, not the feature — rebuild ICP around who achieves the outcome

famous_failures.detail: Solved 'juicing is hard' but the real problem was 'I want healthy juice at home' — solved by cutting the bag with scissors.
Solved 'I want social updates from my contacts' but the real problem was 'I want to know what interesting people say'.

sources: Clayton Christensen 'Jobs to be Done' framework

name: Refund Rate Destruction
narrative: Consumer product refund rates exceed 20-30%. Every third customer who buys returns it. The gross merchandise value looks strong but net revenue after refunds is half the headline number. Payment processors begin holding reserves. Shopify or Amazon penalises accounts with high refund rates. The true unit economics only become clear when refund costs are attributed per customer — at which point the business model is revealed as structurally unviable.

warning_signals.signal: Refund rate above 15% of gross revenue
Payment processor beginning to hold reserves or requesting explanations
Reviews citing 'not what was advertised' or 'returned immediately'
Chargeback rate above 0.5% (Visa threshold for monitoring programme)
Customer acquisition campaigns attracting high-refund demographics

survival_playbook: Calculate your true unit economics including refunds — the number may be a shock
Diagnose refund reasons: unmet expectations (marketing problem) vs. product quality (build problem)
Fix marketing first: over-promising to drive conversions then facing refunds is a compounding trap
Implement a 30-day customer success check-in for every new customer before the refund window
Reduce refund window to the minimum legally allowed — remove friction from keeping, add friction from returning

famous_failures.detail: Consumer products refund economics combined with Amazon's response pricing destroyed unit economics.
High-refund consumer health products with subscription models — chargeback rates triggered payment processor action.

name: API-First Without Developer Experience
narrative: The company built a technically capable API but ignored developer experience. Documentation is incomplete, error messages are cryptic, the SDK has bugs, and it takes 3 days to get to hello world. Developers evaluate in the first 30 minutes and move on. Word of mouth in the developer community is extremely fast and extremely negative. The product's technical merit is irrelevant — developers won't use what they can't figure out.

warning_signals.signal: Average time-to-first-API-call exceeding 4 hours
Documentation quality rated poorly in developer surveys
GitHub issues full of 'how do I' questions that should be in docs
Developer community forum negative about setup experience
Trial-to-paid conversion significantly below developer API benchmarks (15%+)

survival_playbook: Hire a Developer Relations engineer whose only job is to make the first 30 minutes magical
Record yourself doing a first-time setup from scratch — fix every friction point you encounter
Write docs for beginners first, power users second — not the reverse
Create interactive tutorials in the browser: no install required to see the first working example
Benchmark your time-to-hello-world against Stripe and Twilio — those are the DX standards

famous_failures.detail: Developer API products with poor DX consistently lose to Stripe/Twilio/Plaid who invested in developer experience.
PayPal's terrible developer experience nearly lost the market to Stripe. 'PayPal mafia' went to Stripe.

name: Channel Partner Cannibalization
narrative: The company built a successful reseller or channel partner programme. Partners are now generating 60%+ of revenue. Then the company builds a direct sales team that competes with its own channel for the same customers. Partners feel betrayed and start evaluating competing products. Channel revenue collapses while the direct team isn't yet mature enough to replace it. The company loses both channels simultaneously.

warning_signals.signal: Channel partners inquiring about direct team competition for their accounts
Partner-attributed deal registration declining month-over-month
Top partners starting to offer competing products alongside yours
Direct team closing deals that partners previously brought to you
Channel revenue declining while direct revenue is still nascent

survival_playbook: Establish explicit rules of engagement before building direct team — protect registered partner deals
Separate market territories: channel owns SMB, direct owns enterprise (or vice versa)
Create a partner advisory board and involve them in the direct team strategy before launch
Compensate the channel for deals they register even if direct closes them — partner trust has value
If channel conflict is unavoidable: do a controlled, announced transition, not a passive drift

famous_failures.detail: Documented channel conflict as company moved from channel-only to direct. Took 2 years to stabilise.
Gartner documents channel cannibalization as #2 GTM failure mode for technology companies with resellers.

name: Advisor Equity Without Accountability
narrative: The company gave equity to 10+ advisors in the first year. They were well-intentioned at signing but provide minimal ongoing value. Critical introductions never materialised. Quarterly calls became monthly, then stopped. The cap table is now cluttered with 2-5% total advisor equity with no accountability mechanism. When the company needs help most, the advisors are too busy or too removed from current challenges to assist.

warning_signals.signal: Advisors not responding to introduction requests within 2 weeks
Advisor meetings cancelled more than twice in a row
No attributable value (deals, hires, press) from advisors in past 6 months
Total advisor equity above 3% with nothing to show
New investors asking why advisors haven't helped with introductions

survival_playbook: Include performance clauses in advisor agreements: specific deliverables or vest forfeiture
Annual advisor review: did they deliver? If not, have the conversation to part ways gracefully
Cap total advisor equity at 1% of fully diluted shares
Prefer one highly engaged advisor over five passive ones — quality over quantity
Advisors should vest on a 2-year schedule with no cliff — vesting on delivery, not time

famous_failures.detail: First Round Capital estimates 60% of advisor equity produces no measurable return for the company.

famous_failures.company: Multiple named advisors to failed startups
famous_failures.detail: High-profile advisor lists on failed startups regularly cited in post-mortems as validation that didn't materialise.

sources: First Round Capital advisor programme best practices

name: Pricing Page Anxiety Paralysis
narrative: The founders are afraid to show prices publicly. The pricing page says 'Contact Us' for all tiers. This creates friction, delays the sales cycle by weeks, and filters out the self-serve SMB market entirely. Prospects who won't fill in a contact form never enter the funnel. The company underestimates how many customers would buy immediately at the right price with no salesperson required. Hiding pricing is hiding from the market.

warning_signals.signal: More than 50% of deals requiring a sales call before pricing is discussed
Trial-to-paid conversion below 5% — pricing friction is killing conversions
Sales team spending majority of calls on pricing discovery, not value
Website analytics showing high bounce rate on pricing page
Self-serve competitors winning SMB deals without a sales call

survival_playbook: Publish transparent pricing immediately — test 3 price points with A/B testing
Add a free trial or freemium tier that lets customers experience value before pricing conversation
Move all deals below $10K ACV to fully self-serve — no sales call required
Measure sales cycle length before and after publishing pricing — it almost always shortens
Competitors with public pricing are not at a disadvantage — they are at a speed advantage

famous_failures.company: Many enterprise SaaS in PLG era
famous_failures.outcome: Lost SMB market to PLG competitors
famous_failures.detail: Companies that refused to show pricing lost entire self-serve segments to Notion, Linear, Figma.
Salesforce-era 'call us for pricing' model disrupted by HubSpot transparent pricing in CRM.

name: Copycat Without Differentiation
narrative: The founder saw a successful product in another market (often the US), replicated it for a different geography or segment, and assumed the model would translate. The copycat lacks the original's network effects, brand, and first-mover advantages. The original eventually enters the market. The copycat has no differentiation to defend. Customers migrate to the original or to a lower-cost alternative.

warning_signals.signal: Sales team unable to articulate differentiation vs. original product
Original company announcing entry into target market
Customers referring to the original product as the gold standard
Win rate declining as original product improves localisation
Price pressure intensifying as both original and lower-cost alternatives compete

survival_playbook: Identify the one thing the original product will never build — build that as your differentiation
Go deeper on local market nuances: language, regulation, cultural workflow differences
Build distribution advantages the original cannot replicate: local partnerships, government relationships
Acquire customers before the original arrives — switching costs are your moat
Consider a partnership or white-label arrangement with the original — becoming their local partner

famous_failures.detail: Airbnb clone for Europe. Airbnb entered Europe. Wimdu sold to competitor HouseTrip.
Groupon clone. Groupon entered Europe, acquired them rather than compete.

name: Enterprise Proof of Concept Trap
narrative: The company is running 8 concurrent enterprise proof-of-concepts. Each one took 3 months to negotiate and is now in month 4 of a promised 2-month evaluation. None have converted to paid. The enterprise procurement process is using free POCs as a substitute for actual evaluation criteria. The sales team is managing POCs instead of closing. Revenue pipeline looks full but cash is draining.

warning_signals.signal: More than 3 concurrent POCs running simultaneously
POC average duration extending beyond 3 months
POC-to-paid conversion rate below 25%
Enterprise champion unable to name a specific decision date
Same POC being renewed instead of converted

survival_playbook: Never start a POC without a signed Mutual Action Plan with specific success criteria and a go/no-go date
Charge for POCs — even $5K creates commitment and filters out non-serious evaluations
Set a hard cap: 60 days maximum for any POC — force a decision or close the deal
Qualify the champion: do they have budget authority? If not, find who does before starting
Kill POCs that have slipped past deadline without a renewed commitment in writing

famous_failures.detail: AI companies reported 80%+ POC pipeline with under 10% conversion. 'POC purgatory' became industry term.
Enterprise security POC cycles of 6-18 months with low conversion trapped dozens of security startups.

name: Acqui-hire Positioning Trap
narrative: The company stops trying to build a product and starts positioning itself for acqui-hire. Features slow down. The team focuses on looking attractive to acquirers. Product development stalls. The best engineers — who joined to build something — sense the shift and leave for companies still trying to win. The remaining team is less attractive than before. Acquirers pay far less for a team in decline than a team in momentum.

warning_signals.signal: CEO spending time on acquisition conversations vs. product conversations
Engineering hiring freeze while business development hires continue
Top engineers announcing departures for product-stage companies
Product roadmap effectively frozen for 90+ days
Investor updates shifting from metrics to 'strategic options'

survival_playbook: Make an explicit decision: build or sell — do not half-do both
If selling: hire an M&A banker and run a structured process, not passive inbound conversations
If building: recommit publicly internally and externally — culture follows conviction
Acqui-hires pay $1-3M per engineer on average — a strong product exits at 10-100x that
The best acqui-hire outcome still requires a functioning, motivated team — protect the team above all else

famous_failures.detail: Used car marketplace shifted to acqui-hire mode. Best engineers left. Acquired by Fair.com for minimal consideration.
Shifted to positioning mode. Team declined. Acqui-hire value was fraction of capital raised.

sources: Ben Horowitz 'The Hard Thing About Hard Things' on acqui-hire dynamics
